Query: After removal of periodontal dressing what should be done to keep sensitivity to the minimum?

Options:
A: Use of mouthwash
B: Use of desensitizing paste
C: Keep roots, plaque free
D: None of the above

Document [0] (Title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Complications -- Pocket Reduction Surgeries) Teeth with exposed root surfaces may develop dentinal hypersensitivity, causing sharp pain in response to cold, hot, or highly osmotic foods. While minor tooth sensitivity is common after most dental procedures, sensitivity after periodontal surgery can be pronounced and may require intervention. Treatment options for dentinal hypersensitivity include over-the-counter and in-office desensitizing agents, the application of sealants, or, in severe cases, root canal therapy.
Document [1] (Title: Periodontal Disease -- Treatment / Management) Antibiotics may be administered locally or systemically in cases that do not respond to mechanical therapy. Chlorhexidine gluconate is a commonly used antimicrobial agent that complements mechanical periodontal treatment. This agent is typically administered as a mouth rinse, but can also be applied as a gel, varnish, or subgingival chip. When used alongside regular toothbrushing, chlorhexidine helps reduce plaque buildup, making it highly effective in treating chronic periodontitis. A relatively new advancement in pharmacotherapy for periodontal disease involves the use of a chlorhexidine gluconate chip, which is inserted into the periodontal pocket after cleaning. This chip provides a long-term, sustained release of chlorhexidine gluconate into the affected area, enhancing treatment effectiveness. [36] [37] [38]

Local anesthesia may be required. Drainage should be accompanied by the mechanical scaling of the periodontal pocket and antiseptic rinse, removing necrotic tissue and bacterial load. This enables the host immune response to tackle the infection.

Document [26] (Title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Technique or Treatment -- Achieving Hemostasis) Following the removal of a tooth, a roll of gauze or bite pack should be placed over the socket, and the patient should be asked to bite together or hold down on the pack firmly. This pressure should be placed for a minimum of five minutes, and hemostasis should be checked before the patient can leave the chair. If hemostasis has not been achieved after a prolonged period of pressure, then local hemostatic agents should be used. The above steps outline the usual sequence of simple removal of a tooth. Should these steps not be successful in removing a tooth, then a surgical approach may be required.

Recognizing that tetracyklin agents are part of the larger Tetracycline Antibiotics family helps clinicians understand their mechanism of action, spectrum of activity, and appropriate therapeutic role in patient care.
Document [22] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1803) Antiinfectives and antiseptics for local oral treatment is a therapeutic category that includes doxycycline and minocyklin. Both doxycycline and minocyklin are members of this class, indicating they are used as local oral anti‑infective and antiseptic agents. Their inclusion in the same category suggests they share similar indications for treating oral infections and can be considered when selecting topical antimicrobial therapy for dental or oral conditions.
Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1838) Charcoal preparations that are used as intestinal absorbents are classified as members of the activated charcoal group.
Document [24] (Title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2052) ENZYMES FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS are classified as a type of PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS. The broader category PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS includes CICATRICANTS, which are agents that promote scar formation and facilitate wound healing. Consequently, enzymes used in wound and ulcer therapy belong to the larger group of cicatricant preparations, highlighting their role within the spectrum of therapeutic agents aimed at promoting tissue repair.
Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1867) Phenol and its derivatives and quaternary ammonium compounds are both classified as types of antiseptics and disinfectants, belonging to the broader category of agents used to inhibit or destroy microorganisms on living tissues and inanimate surfaces. As members of the antiseptics and disinfectants group, these chemical families share the common purpose of preventing infection and maintaining hygiene in clinical and community settings.
Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 674) formulations include chlorhexidine / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo, which integrates an antiseptic with a skin‑conditioning lipid, and salicylic acid 18 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo, providing a focused keratolytic action. Collectively, these diverse Medicated Shampoo dose forms illustrate a comprehensive approach to treating scalp infections, inflammation, and hyperkeratosis by tailoring active ingredient combinations and concentrations to specific therapeutic goals.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1828) Acyclovir 50 mg/mL topical cream is classified as a topical antiviral medication, belonging to the broader category of Antivirals, topical.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72) The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1989) Corticosteroids for local oral treatment and other agents for local oral treatment in ATC are both classified as stomatological preparations, indicating that each is a type of medication formulated for use within the oral cavity to manage dental or mucosal conditions.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 43) although they are not directly linked to ketoconazole’s contraindication profile. Collectively, the extensive variety of ketoconazole and chlorhexidine‑based medicated shampoos—tailored by specific active‑ingredient combinations and concentrations—provides targeted antimicrobial, antifungal, anti‑inflammatory, keratolytic, moisturizing, antipruritic, and analgesic therapy for scalp and skin disorders, while vigilant awareness of drug‑drug interactions and contraindicated drug classes is essential for optimal patient safety.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2386) جرح قطع (cut wound) is classified as a specific type of جرح مفتوح (open wound), indicating that cut wounds are a subset of open wounds and share the characteristic of tissue exposure that requires appropriate assessment and management.
